speaker: interviewer
text: Take a glass-- a drink of water?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I'm fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're fine?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm, I'm fine. You've heard of Cold-EEZE?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You haven't heard of Cold-EEZE?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: The magic, new, anti-cold formula?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it working for you?

speaker: interviewer
text: It is fabulous.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. OK. I'm asking you to wait for 20 more seconds, and then we're ready. I'm letting the bars go. And then we'll be in great shape. This gives you the chance to be perfectly relaxed. This is our quiet time together. And then we're ready to go. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: January 31, 1997. The survivor is Paul Wos. His name in Poland was Pawel Wos. I'm interviewing him. My name is Anne Bernard. The interview is taking place in Seacliff, New York, the United States of America. And the interview is taking place in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: January 31, 1997. The survivor is Paul Wos. He was born as Pawel Wos. I'm interviewing him. My name is Anne Bernard. The interview is taking place in Seacliff, New York, the United States of America. And the interview is taking place in English. Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Paul Wos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell Wos?

speaker: interviewee
text: W-O-S. In Polish, there was an apostrophe over the S. It was 'Vosh.'

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pawel Zenon Wos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-A-W-E-L, Pawel, Zenon, Z-E-N-N-E-N, and then W-O-S, Wos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a nickname?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zenon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did most of the family call you Zeon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zenon, Zenon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Zenon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Zenon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I born in Warsaw, January 1st, 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: 76, probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father name was also Pawel. Grandfather also Pawel. That mean the Pawel III, I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother first name is-- was Anna. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] family name was Szachowska, Szachowski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's not so easy. S-Z-A-C-H-O-W-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, was family of four children. I was the oldest one. Second was Irina. And third was my brother, Odon. And the fourth was Wanda, the youngest one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the difference in age between each of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Between me and my sister was two years, between my sister and brother three years, and between brother and sister, again, two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: This time, I mean, 1920s, my father was working for a Vistula River navy. That was years-- years ago. And my father was, like, I can say, sailor. Is right in English? And before the-- working for state, was, during the First War, prisoner of the war, together, my mother, too. During-- in 1916-- '18, after the war-- First War break, the German capture my father and mother and grandparents, because was going by Vistula River to Gdansk, Danzig, to port and supposed to go to America. That was before 1918. And was captured, because my father and my mother was live in the gubernia, Russian part of the Poland. Going through the German part of the Poland, in the Vistula River, was capture-- I mean is arrested and sent to a place, where, during the war, four years, was working on the farm, big farm [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the west part of the Poland. And after the war was ended, my father and my mother meet together, at this time, in the camp and come back to Warsaw, married. And I was the first child. And my father was working for state [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] company, mean was working like a sailor in-- on the ship on Vistula River.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do for the rest of his life in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- my father was working later there in state, big place, where they all was built, the ships. And that was a couple years. And later, there start a small business, small factory. That was the latest time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How late was it when he started the small factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 19-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --30, 1940, something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in Warsaw, in a part, the old part of the old town, very close to the king castle in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe where you lived, the apartment in which you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, apartment was like, you know, this time people like my father start organize own life. And this was other child, like Irina and also the next one. And the apartment was not big. Was two and-- two-- three-- three rooms and kitchen, something like this. And we was small family. Save the money. Probably, that's my father was doing. And that was one of the nicer part of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way was it nice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we was close to the Vistula River. Each evening, after we grow a little bit up, we go with our parents on the Vistula boardwalk. And if I look back, that was one of the nicer part of my memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of relationship did you have with your father and mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. Very good. My father was very honest man. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Cared about the family. Very ambition. Very handsome. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] helped people if he found it possible. And everybody liked my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a relationship did you have with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Anna probably was the best mother. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What are some of your favorite memories of your family together?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Oh, something vacation, small vacation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We was together, the four-- four of us, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the place where, you know, was very nice scenery, near the Vistula River, maybe 50 kilometer from Warsaw. We spent, one time, a very nice vacation and four children, father. And that was one of the nicer time in my life. And still, we was working very hard, I mean our parents. Sometime my father took-- took two jobs to support the family. And was all the time successful and was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a person who can work and with everybody and has very good reputation, like man who you can trust. My mother, in her house, was a little bit firm, streng, keep the family together. For example, father never beat the children. Mother-- [LAUGHS] sometimes with stuff. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And was very good person.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a child were you? What kind of a child were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think so-- I was-- I was all right. My parents has not problem with me, in other words. What kind of child I was? I don't know. [LAUGHS] I know only opinion. I was very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] quiet and listen parents. That's parents' opinion not mine. And in the school, for example, in Poland, this time, up to '39, was very hard support for children in a school. Because tuition, in the school, in gymnasium, was very, very costable-- costs money. And I was in a school, in a state school, a gymnasium, where I pay only not 65 zloty. I pay only 13, because my mark was good. In other words, my part was help family. My brother and sister, for example, my father pay 85 zloty per child, by my sister and my son-- my brother. And for me pay only $13, because I have the boat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Irina and Odon went to private school. That costs much more money and was very good school. In Poland, this time, was practice, if somebody can, and the child is good, send to the state school. And supposed to be the program, supposed to be a little bit better in state school. Myself, I not believe. Private school gymnasium was very, very good. If I can, right now, go back and think what my children, here, in United State, learn during high school and what we learn, I see two years' different in studying. I mean, you finish gymnasium in Poland, before the war, mean you know much more like my children after finish high school here. But that's private-- private opinion, you know. And that's everybody like have the children in state school-- I mean gymnasium, because it costs less and the education was very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of your favorite subjects?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my favorites? That's, you know, a very interesting question. I go to technical way, technical subjects. And I was all the time good and good mathematician. Only my favor was geography, travel. And it's what I am doing, you know, now. If I can, I like go all over the world to where I never was before. And during the-- my high school time, for me was the best geographic, economy, and this relation subject. That was my-- years ago, this time, I was in high school. We was very strict require. For example, in some level of-- in my school, I must know all rivers in Europe, all town in-- I mean, DC, like Washington, DC, all words.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people in each country, generally speaking? What-- in other, from economical point of view, what each country produce and export? And all the highest mountain in the world, how many feet or meters, something like this. That was very interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember, I receive one very interesting [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] reward about prepare the maps. My hands was very good to prepare the maps. And that was my-- I was very patient, good graphic. I put nice letter, for example. And how many times-- how many years, I remember, in my life, that was true reward for me. All drawings, technical drawings, all the time, I was the first in the class. And also, the maps, for example, that was my-- and also, during the school, if I can say so, I was-- up to now, I have the same feeling. People trust me. I was all the time in school secretary of the money, financing secretary. How many time-- years, I remember. All the time, people vote for me. Pawel be the guy. [LAUGHS] Now, I am here in a Polish museum also eight years trustee and financing guy. I don't know, for public, people [LAUGHS] don't know me. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the high school. And what I can tell about the high school? During the high school time was also for me nice. Because I was very good gymnastic, sportsman.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- sometime my father was very upset, because, in this country, if you are good sportsman, you have career. There, you-- if you are good sportsman, you can die without the bread. [LAUGHS] This time, and that was 50, 60 years ago. And I was good in gymnastic, the same, my brother, very good. And also, during the-- up to 1938, I was in team. We played the basketball. I was short, small, and still I was good shooter. [LAUGHS] And also, the other-- other subject, sport subject, that was the rowing on the skinny boat, eight people plus the fellow who's steering. I was on the top of that. That was my pleasure. And we go sometime from Warsaw up to the stream, 15, 20 kilometers, and back [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to Warsaw. That was my fabor-- favor sports subject. That's during the academic time. And I was sometime upset my children was not so good. [LAUGHS] I wish-- you know, Konrad finished Princeton and Cornell and never was in the-- some sports involvement. That's, I think, for young people is very good and a very important thing. That was my time in the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of social relationships did you have with friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Generally speaking, I have good relationship, because-- mostly, because I was, all the time, on the top in the high school in sport. Everybody like being friend with me. Mostly people go to me, not I go to people. And I would never have special problem with friends, with the professor or something like this. I was all right. I-- maybe, I was not all all that right, and still people try and be friendly with me. No problem with a relationship between the students and professor, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any Jewish friends during your school years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I was young, was one-- in my class was one Jewish fellow. Because mostly was people Catholic and Protestant. Maybe 4 Protestant, not 25, 30 people, and 1 Jewish, Wodka. And young fellow, from poor family, and later, after 14 years, our-- my age, 14, go to Palestine. This time was Palestine, I know. Was-- he was, you know, friendly, like, with everybody. And later there, they-- we have a relationship with the Jewish people, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1936, '37, up to Warsaw Ghetto was collapse. That was not school children and not-- not student, only a relationship because my father was involved in business with the Jewish people in Warsaw. That's son or brother or something. This was this kind relationship up to '30-- '39. After was different story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us what religion you practiced? What religion did you practice at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am Catholic. I am Catholic. My mother was a very strict Catholic. In other words, we traditionally doing everything what's supposed to be. Catholic religion is not complicated, you know? [LAUGHS] You  must only go by the Ten Amendment, that's all. And we go each Sunday to church and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] thank God for what happened last week and ask for better next week. And my father was all the time generous and put something for the poor. That's--

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how often did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we go each Sunday, each Sunday. And I think so that was very good, because this way is some line for young people, like we was. If-- I know other children who was not so strict-- I am not saying we was-- my father and mother was strict. Only we have some program. Sunday, we go to church. After church, we go do something, pleasure. And was nice not only go to the church, only, after the church, my father was very big esteta. Mean was dressed nice, clean, interesting everything what was built around him, and good taste from architecture point of view. And also, was interesting what going on around us. And we go sometime to park, where are, you know, some nice tree and nice birds. And my father know each bird by name. [LAUGHS] Mean-- what I mean, this kind bird's doing this, the other doing the other, and the same with the tree. He now just be like we call today, naturalist, by own feeling. And we grow this way. And we grow in family where we was-- my parents was very tolerance. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Probably because was four years in camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. We'll continue in the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: 30 minute?
